coredata.dc:title: Decontamination of emerging pharmaceutical pollutants using carbon-dots as robust materials
coredata.dc:creator: González-González, Reyna Berenice
Sharma, Ashutosh
Parra-Saldívar, Roberto
Ramirez-Mendoza, Ricardo A.
Bilal, Muhammad
Iqbal, Hafiz M.N.
coredata.dc:description: Environmental pollution is a critical issue that requires proper measures to maintain environmental health in a sustainable and effective manner. The growing persistence of several active pharmaceutical residues, such as antibiotics like tetracycline, and anti-inflammatory drugs like diclofenac in water matrices is considered an issue of global concern. Numerous sewage/drain waste lines from the domestic and pharmaceutical sector contain an array of toxic compounds, so-called “emerging pollutants” and possess adverse effects on entire living ecosystem and damage its biodiversity. Therefore, effective solution and preventive measures are urgently required to sustainably mitigate and/or remediate pharmaceutically active emerging pollutants from environmental matrices. In this context, herein, the entry pathways of the pharmaceutical waste into the environment are presented, through the entire lifecycle of a pharmaceutical product. There is no detailed review available on carbon-dots (CDs) as robust materials with multifunctional features that support sustainable mitigation of emerging pollutants from water matrices. Thus, CDs-based photocatalysts are emerging as an efficient alternative for decontamination by pharmaceutical pollutants. The addition of CDs on photocatalytic systems has an important role in their performance, mainly because of their up-conversion property, transfer photoinduced electron capacities, and efficient separation of electrons and holes. In this review, we analyze the strategies followed by different researchers to optimize the photodegradation of various pharmaceutical pollutants. In this manner, the effect of different parameters such as pH, the dosage of photocatalyst, amount of carbon dots, and initial pollutant concentration, among others are discussed. Finally, current challenges are presented from a pollution prevention perspective and from CDs-based photocatalytic remediation perspective, with the aim to suggest possible research directions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental pollution is a critical issue that requires proper measures to maintain environmental health in a sustainable and effective manner. The growing persistence of several active pharmaceutical residues, such as antibiotics like tetracycline, and anti-inflammatory drugs like diclofenac in water matrices is considered an issue of global concern. Numerous sewage/drain waste lines from the domestic and pharmaceutical sector contain an array of toxic compounds, so-called “emerging pollutants” and possess adverse effects on entire living ecosystem and damage its biodiversity. Therefore, effective solution and preventive measures are urgently required to sustainably mitigate and/or remediate pharmaceutically active emerging pollutants from environmental matrices. In this context, herein, the entry pathways of the pharmaceutical waste into the environment are presented, through the entire lifecycle of a pharmaceutical product. There is no detailed review available on carbon-dots (CDs) as robust materials with multifunctional features that support sustainable mitigation of emerging pollutants from water matrices. Thus, CDs-based photocatalysts are emerging as an efficient alternative for decontamination by pharmaceutical pollutants. The addition of CDs on photocatalytic systems has an important role in their performance, mainly because of their up-conversion property, transfer photoinduced electron capacities, and efficient separation of electrons and holes. In this review, we analyze the strategies followed by different researchers to optimize the photodegradation of various pharmaceutical pollutants. In this manner, the effect of different parameters such as pH, the dosage of photocatalyst, amount of carbon dots, and initial pollutant concentration, among others are discussed. Finally, current challenges are presented from a pollution prevention perspective and from CDs-based photocatalytic remediation perspective, with the aim to suggest possible research directions.

ce:section.ce:para: Undoubtedly, pharmaceutical products have had a critical role in human health and well-being. However, like any other human-made product, they eventually become waste, and if their disposition is inadequate can also become a problem of global concern. The presence of pharmaceutical products had been detected in aquatic environments including groundwater and surface water (Bexfield et al., 2019; Kim and Carlson, 2006; Watkinson et al., 2009). Their impacts on environmental and human health have been studied, and although their effects still unclear, pharmaceutical pollutants are already recognized as contaminants of emerging concern (Hernández et al., 2019; Massima Mouele et al., 2021; Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). For example, antibiotics released to the environment may cause ﻿long-term and irreversible effects to microorganisms ﻿by causing genetic exchange, thus accelerating the resistance of pathogens against a wide range of antibiotics (Abdel-Shafy and Mohamed-Mansour, 2013; Bagheri et al., 2017). Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) caused by antibiotics’ misused and their release into the environment, is a critical issue in medicine with relevant impacts on the economy and population’s health (Shalini et al., 2010).

Some of the characteristics that differentiate pharmaceutical pollutants from conventional pollutants and complicate their removal are: (a) they present wide variations on molecular weight, structure, functionality, and shape; (b) they are polar molecules with ionizable ﻿groups, which properties are highly dependent on the pH of the medium, and (c) after consumption and absorption, pharmaceutical molecules undergo metabolic reactions which modify their chemical structure (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). Experimental difficulties in the understanding of the interaction of pharmaceutical compounds with the environment might be found due to the molecular complexity of pharmaceutical compounds based on variations on their properties, such as their stability, solubility, polarity, and ionization, which also depend on the environmental properties. Table 1 shows the chemical and safety information of pharmaceutical compounds commonly used, some of them reporting toxicity to aquatic environments.

Different conventional treatment systems are used for decontamination by conventional pollutants, such as adsorption or activated sludge treatment (Jin et al., 2011; Su et al., 2021). Unfortunately, conventional systems are not effective enough to completely ﻿remove pharmaceutical molecules (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). Therefore, a proper degradation of these emerging pollutants required the development of new technologies and materials, which consequently has led to the exploration of the use of carbon nanomaterials (Jun et al., 2018). The unique and fascinating properties of carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon nanomaterial, have caused an increased number of publications in recent years ( Fig. 1a). However, the most widely used carbon nanomaterials for water remediation are carbon nanotubes and graphene-based nanomaterials (Fig. 1b). A total of 2053 scientific publications related to the use of graphene and its application in wastewater remediation have been published from 2012 to 2020 according to a search performed on Scopus database; 1112 publications associated with the use of carbon nanotubes; and 249 scientific articles related to CDs. From the latter number, only 12 review articles were found including information on the CDs applied on wastewater treatment (Fig. 1c); however, a focused review on wastewater decontamination from pharmaceutical pollutants using CDs remains unavailable in the literature.

In this review, we aim to focus on the identification of entry pathways for pharmaceutical products into the environment followed by the analysis of the most recent advances and applications of CDs-based materials for decontamination of pharmaceutical products. With this purpose, the main properties of CDs-based materials and the effects on the pollutant ﻿removal capacity of the most relevant parameters, are resumed. Finally, current challenges both in the proper management of pharmaceutical products and in the use of CDs-based materials for pharmaceutical pollutants decontamination are discussed.

In Section 3, we briefly introduce pharmaceutical pollutants in a global context and their environmental concern. Then, Section 4 presents a general description of the main conventional systems for the treatment of pharmaceutical pollutants. In Section 5, we thoroughly review recent studies on CDs-based photocatalyst applied on pharmaceutical pollutants removal and the effect on the photocatalytic activity of the main parameters. Then, in Section 6 are discussed the current challenges from two different perspectives: pharmaceutical pollution prevention and CDs-based photocatalyst. Finally, conclusions are presented in Section 7. The overall work layout is shown in Fig. 2.

A literature search was performed with different databases such as “Google Scholar”, “Science Direct”, “Scopus”, and “Web of Science” including combinations of keywords like “carbon dots”, “carbon quantum dots”, “graphene quantum dots”, “removal”, “pharmaceutical” “pollutants”, “wastewater”, “remediation”. Studies found in the literature were collected and investigated. In addition, their reference lists were explored to expand the selection to a greater number of publications. A large number of scientific articles focused on the degradation of other types of pollutants different from pharmaceuticals were excluded. The process applied to remove the pharmaceutical waste was not considered as exclusion criteria; however, only photocatalysis applications were found, contrary to carbon nanotubes that are frequently applied in adsorption and catalysis processes on environmental remediation applications. The remaining studies were collected and carefully investigated.

Pharmaceuticals are a group of organic compounds manufactured for their use as a medicinal drug to treat illnesses, which have had a positive impact on longevity and human health. Pharmaceutical innovations such as penicillin, streptomycin, and vaccines are attributed to the dramatic improvement in life expectancy after 1940 (Mishra, 2016). Access to high-quality, effective, safe, and affordable medicines for the global population is also relevant; therefore, pharmaceutical companies and governments are developing initiatives to increase the accessibility and affordability of medicines for all (Access to Medicine Foundation, 2008). The importance and undoubted benefits of the pharmaceutical industry place it among the world’s wealthiest industries (Nyagah et al., 2020). In 2019, the global pharmaceutical manufacturing market size was valued at USD 324.42 billion, which is expected to grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 13.74% from 2020 to 2027 (Grand View Research, 2020). Similarly, global medicine consumption has been increasing at 3% (CAGR) since 2014 (IQVIA Institute, 2020). Nevertheless, the expanding pharmaceutical industry emerges as a worldwide public health concern, since higher sales and use of medicines represent greater amounts of chemicals entering the environment (Ara et al., 2021; Nyagah et al., 2020; Taoufik et al., 2020; Tiwari et al., 2017). The pharmaceutical residues have been found in almost all aquatic environments around the world such as surface water and groundwater (Bexfield et al., 2019; Kim and Carlson, 2006; Watkinson et al., 2009).

Worldwide, approximately 4000 different active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) are manufactured, incorporated into tens of thousands of pharmaceutical preparations available in the market (Daughton, 2007, 2014). However, during the manufacturing process of APIs, not only the ﻿final product can become a waste, but also other materials and liquids are contaminated, which increases the waste volume (Kümmerer, 2008). In this manner, the pharmaceutical pollutants include: chemical or biological pharmaceutical products for human or veterinary medical purposes, either unused or excreted by the human or animal body; residues or wastes from manufacturing processes presented in any of the common physical forms (solids, liquids, or gases), such as solvents used as a reaction medium or for purification, and; waste medical items that were in contact with pharmaceutical products such as vials, bottles, and bags (Nyagah et al., 2020).

Among the large variety of pharmaceutical products, antibiotics represent a considerable environmental ﻿hazard to aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems (Bagheri et al., 2017). For many years, antibiotics have demonstrated their benefits as a ﻿therapeutic treatment for ﻿diseases in humans and animals (Kim and Carlson, 2006). In recent years, antibiotics have received ﻿increased attention because of their environmental impact when they reach the environment. The antimicrobial resistance (AMR) caused by the misuse and improper disposal of antibiotics used for medical purposes, as well as its excessive usage on animals to enhance growth and feed efficiency (Bagheri et al., 2017; Kim and Carlson, 2006), provides selective pressure favoring the appearance of resistant strains (Lerbech et al., 2014). In this context, AMR is nowadays recognized by the World Health Organization (WHO) as a major global health problem (WHO, 2014).

Globally, antibiotics are the most frequently prescribed pharmaceuticals, and their consumption has been increasing caused by the recent higher accessibility and affordability (CDDEP, 2021). Between 2000 and 2015, antibiotic consumption increased 91%, while the increase was much higher in low- and middle-income countries, reaching 165% (CDDEP, 2021). The antimicrobial consumption in animals is higher than in humans (Bagheri et al., 2017; CDDEP, 2021; Van et al., 2020). In many developed countries, 50−80% of the total produced antibiotics, are used on livestock (Cully, 2014). Worldwide, only livestock consumed 63,151 tons of antimicrobials in 2010, which is projected to reach 105,596 tons by 2030 (Van Boeckel et al., 2015). Antibiotic use on aquaculture is also relevant since aquaculture consumption is increasing even faster than meat and dairy consumption. In this sector, global antimicrobial consumption of 13,600 tons is estimated by 2030 (CDDEP, 2021).

The main pathways by which pharmaceutical products and their residues can reach the environment are: (1) wastewater discharged from pharmaceutical manufacturing plants; (2) domestic and hospital effluents containing pharmaceutical compounds excreted in the urine and feces of humans; (3) agricultural effluents containing pharmaceutical compounds excreted in the urine and feces of animals; and (4) improper disposal of unused or expired pharmaceutical products through the sink, toilet or in a general waste bin (aus der Beek et al., 2016; O’Flynn et al., 2021; Patel et al., 2019; Tiwari et al., 2017; Veiga-Gómez et al., 2017). Other pathways have been reported, such as the applications of pharmaceutical substances directly to water for aquaculture and wastewater irrigation of agriculture (aus der Beek et al., 2016; Patel et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pharmaceutical pollution trough the lifecycle of a pharmaceutical product
ce:section.ce:para: The life cycle of a pharmaceutical product consists of three main stages: manufacturing, consumption, and waste disposal. Each stage of the cycle is an important route for the entry of pharmaceutical pollutants into the environment (Kusturica et al., 2020).

The manufacturing of pharmaceutical products is divided into three main processes: 1) research and development (R&D); 2) API manufacturing, which can be achieved by chemical synthesis or natural product extraction and; 3) the formulation of the final drug product, which is when the API is formulated and mixed with excipients and additives (Dunn et al., 2010; Shalini et al., 2010).

The R&D department in the pharmaceutical industry includes chemical, microbiological, and pharmacological research. Therefore, chemical and biological wastes are produced being the most common halogenated and non-halogenated solvents, bases, and oxidizers (Shalini et al., 2010).

The wastes generated during the API manufacturing through chemical synthesis depends on the pharmaceutical process used and the number of stages involving chemical reactions. The chemical synthesis consists of chemical reactions, followed by separations and washing processes such as extraction and filtration that frequently requires additional amounts of solvents; then, intermediates are isolated through crystallization and the solvents used on previous stages are almost completely removed becoming waste; then, a subsequent purification process is frequently performed such as recrystallization or centrifugation, and finally; the intermediates and final products are dried releasing additional wastes (Dunn et al., 2010; United States Environmental Protection Agency, 1997). Each stage of the API manufacturing process releases wastes including liquid and vapor solvents and wastewater containing intermediates, APIs, solvents, organic and inorganic reactants, and catalysts (Dunn et al., 2010; Shalini et al., 2010).

Pharmaceutical products from natural material sources are produced by natural product extraction processes. The commercial synthesis of extractive pharmaceuticals is complex and expensive, thus natural product extraction is not as frequent as chemical synthesis (United States Environmental Protection Agency, 1998). Natural extraction processes generate wastes including residual raw materials, water-soluble solvents, solvent vapors, and wastewater containing detergents and disinfectants used in cleaning operations (Shalini et al., 2010; United States Environmental Protection Agency, 1998). The total weight of wastes is almost equal to the initial weight of raw materials since APIs extracted are generally presented at very low concentrations in the raw materials (United States Environmental Protection Agency, 1998).

On the other hand, the final drug manufacturing process is of less environmental concern (Dunn et al., 2010). During this process, the APIs are converted to a dosage form for comfortable consumer use, typically in compressed solids (tablets), uncompressed solids (capsules), and liquid formulations, among others (Dunn et al., 2010). In general, few amounts of waste are generated during the final drug manufacturing, mainly small amounts of contaminated wastewater used for cooling and equipment washing operations (United States Environmental Protection Agency, 1998).

For many years, the contribution to the environmental pharmaceutical pollution from manufacturing plants has been considered negligible, based on the assumption that the high value of pharmaceutical products prevents their release into the environment for economic reasons (Hester and Harrison, 2015; Larsson, 2014). However, pharmaceutical pollution caused by manufacturing plants has been observed and identified as significant local sources (Taylor and Senac, 2014). In different parts of the world, drug manufacturing plants have released manufacturing discharges inappropriately, contaminating rivers and lakes (Fick et al., 2009; Holm et al., 1995; Larsson, 2014; Rutgersson et al., 2014). For example, Lübbert et al. (2017) found high concentrations of antibiotics on all environmental samples from 28 different sampling sites collected from the surroundings of drug manufacturing facilities, including the vicinity of two sewage treatment plants and rivers. Similarly, two different studies (Fick et al., 2009; Larsson et al., 2007) reported the presence of extremely high concentrations of pharmaceutical pollutants on samples collected on a wastewater treatment plant situated in Patancheru, India, which receives 1500 m3 of wastewater per day, mainly from drug manufacturers (Fick et al., 2009; Larsson et al., 2007). These studies demonstrate that drug manufacturers are the most important point sources for environmental pharmaceutical contamination in specific locations (Larsson et al., 2007).

Pharmaceuticals administrated on human or veterinary medicine are biologically active chemicals that can be metabolically transformed on the body, through enzymatic systems (Abdel-Shafy and Mohamed-Mansour, 2013). These compounds have an expected effect on the body; however, the excretion of the unmetabolized product and its effects on the environment are typically not considered during their manufacturing. In general, metabolic transformations occur in two phases. Phase I involves oxidation, hydrolysis, and reduction reactions that transform the pharmaceutical product into products with higher polarity and water solubility. The pharmaceutical product can be excreted after this phase if it is already reasonably water-soluble. During phase II, the resulted products from phase I or the unchanged pharmaceutical product are modified by conjugation reactions, in which metabolites are more easily excreted or degraded enzymatically (Burkina et al., 2015; Croom, 2012; Patel et al., 2019).

The metabolization of a pharmaceutical product can significantly vary depending on the physicochemical properties of the product, such as its molecular size and lipophilicity, and biological factors such as pharmacogenetics or ethnic variations (Taxak and Charatam, 2014). Typically, 30 − 90% of an ﻿orally administrated dose, either on humans or animals, are not completely metabolized and excreted as an ﻿active substance (Nyagah et al., 2020; O’Flynn et al., 2021; Tasho and Cho, 2016). The excreted dose differs among the large variety of APIs: only a few pharmaceutical products present a moderated metabolism of 30 − 70%; while the large majority are either widely metabolized or widely excreted unchanged (Daughton, 2014). The excretion of pharmaceutical products occurs mainly through urine, in the range of 55 − 80% of the total (with an average of 70%), and partially through feces (30% of the total) (Abdel-Shafy and Mohamed-Mansour, 2013; Hassan et al., 2019). The partial metabolization of pharmaceutical products has been demonstrated in studies showing the presence of antibiotics in human urine (H. Jin et al., 2010; Lerbech et al., 2014) and feces (Steinbakk et al., 1992). On the other hand, pharmaceutical products administrated via topical or transdermal can reach the environment by their release from the skin, for example during washing (Hester and Harrison, 2015).

Like humans, animals do not completely metabolize the administrated drugs. Zhao et al. (2010) reported the presence of antibiotics in animal dung samples collected from different animal species in China. Similar studies have extensively documented the presence of a variety of antibiotics in livestock, poultry, and swine manure samples (Hou et al., 2015; Leal et al., 2012; Li et al., 2013; Pan et al., 2011; Qiao et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2008), being fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, and tetracyclines, the most frequently reported (Van Epps and Blaney, 2016).

To reduce pharmaceutical contamination, each stage of medicine’s life cycle should be considered; however, the disposal process has one of the most important roles in environmental contamination (Kusturica et al., 2020). The largest pharmaceutical contamination contributor is the domestic effluents (aus der Beek et al., 2016), whether from toilet/sink or garbage disposal. This has been demonstrated by a wide variety of studies performed in different countries. For example, Ahmed et al. (2013) evaluated the disposal practices for unused and expired pharmaceutical products in Pakistan and found that only 2% of participants properly disposed of their medicines, returning them to the pharmacy, while the most common disposal practice (83%) was dumping ﻿in a regular dustbin with other common garbage, without any further processing. This is in agreement with other studies that have demonstrated that dumping unused pharmaceuticals with regular garbage is the most common disposal practice, such as studies developed in Brazil (Quadra et al., 2019), Bangladesh (Begum et al., 2021), Afghanistan (Bashaar et al., 2017), and Ireland (Vellinga et al., 2014). In contrast, when the drug formulation is presented in a liquid form, toilet/sink disposal is the preferred practice by consumers (Albaroodi, 2019; Tong et al., 2011). Moreover, Alnahas et al. (2020) summarize the disposal of pharmaceutical products practices from 34 countries and conclude that 72.7 ± 20.5% corresponds to eco-unfriendly practices such as disposal through garbage and sewer.

The disposal of pharmaceuticals in landfills is also not recommended; however, for a long time, they have been the major disposal method used for both, municipal and industrial wastes (Heim et al., 2004). If pharmaceutical products are deposited in landfills, they could undergo degradation, adsorption, or eventually leach into groundwater (Kümmerer, 2008; Musson and Townsend, 2009). This was demonstrated in studies that detected the presence of pharmaceuticals in the effluent from landfills (Heim et al., 2004; Holm et al., 1995; Schwarzbauer et al., 2002) and on municipal solid waste landfills (Ahel et al., 1998).

In Table 2, the lifecycle of a pharmaceutical product is summarized with the main pharmaceutical wastes produced at each stage and their pathways to reach the environment. In this manner, during the entire lifecycle of a pharmaceutical product, different wastes are generated including solvents, oxidizers, APIs, residual raw materials, released dermal products, among others, which eventually reach the environment.

The three main options for disposing of pharmaceuticals are: i) incineration after their recollection through “take-back” strategies; landfilling after their dumping in a regular dustbin with common garbage, and WWTPs after flushing into the sewer system (Bu et al., 2020).

In order to reduce the environmental impacts of pharmaceutic products, they must be properly collected, identified, separated, and disposed. In this manner, the proper management of pharmaceutical waste begins with the collection at “take-back” sites, which mainly consist of pharmacies (Peake et al., 2015). These “take-back” sites act as intermediaries between consumers and facilities that have the proper capacities for destroying pharmaceuticals (Peake et al., 2015). Although the “take-back” strategies are very unpopular in comparison with un-ecofriendly disposal practices (Ahmed et al., 2013; Begum et al., 2021), the recollection of large quantities of returned medicines has been reported (Mackridge and Marriott, 2007).

To minimize pharmaceutical pollution into the environment, the returned medicines –and pharmaceutical waste from manufacturing plants− are preferably destroyed by incineration at high temperatures (Bean et al., 2016; Peake et al., 2015). Incineration consists of a thermal process performed under controlled conditions to convert waste into gases and mineral residues (Zakaria and Labib, 2003). The incineration of pharmaceutical waste must be performed at high temperatures, typically on two chambers and a controlled air environment. The primary chamber receives the waste and operates at less than the required amount of air to improve the combustion efficiency and to reduce emissions to the atmosphere. Then, to complete the combustion of the exhaust gases from the primary chamber, excess air is added in the second chamber operating at a higher temperature (Yacine et al., 2018). The operating conditions such as temperature, residence time, pretreatments, or handling of residual ashes must meet strict emission control standards to destroy pharmaceuticals efficiently (World Health Organization, 1999). Ideally, the incinerator must operate at 1200ºC, with a gas residence time higher than two seconds (Chandrappa and Das, 2012; Tabash et al., 2016; Zakaria and Labib, 2003). In addition, air pollution control devices are also required.

If high-temperature incineration is not available, medium-temperature incineration can be performed. The double-chambered incinerator must reach at least 850ºC with two seconds combustion retention time in the second chamber. To minimize emissions, the pharmaceutical waste treated through this method should be mixed with municipal waste in the ratio of 1:1000, and halogenated compounds should not exceed 1%, to avoid the presence of halogen in the combustion gases (Nyagah et al., 2020; World Health Organization, 1999).

In this manner, incineration treatment presents many advantages such as significant waste volume reduction, complete destruction of wastes, energy recovery, and less use of land in comparison with other methods such as landfills (Ghasemi and Yusuff, 2016; Trinh et al., 2020). However, disadvantages are also presented. If incinerators are not properly designed and operated, they can release particle ﻿matter, acid gases, polynuclear organic matter, among others. This has been demonstrated on a research from Colombia, which evaluated the quality of the emission of different incinerators and found total suspended particles and dioxin emission concentrations (Hoyos et al., 2008). Incinerators not only release ﻿toxic gases into the atmosphere but also produce solid ash that contains high concentrations of heavy metals (Nyagah et al., 2020; Sabiha-Javied et al., 2008). In this regard, ﻿Sabiha-Javied et al. (2008) found concentrations of Cd, Cr, Cu, Pb, and Zn in ash samples collected from incinerators located in Pakistan. In addition to the ash residues and toxic gases emissions, the high costs associated with the incinerators facilities –operating or transportation costs– lead them to be located mainly in industrialized countries (World Health Organization, 1999); therefore, the capacity of incineration is inadequate and many countries do not have access to properly design and operate.

From an environmental perspective, the best option to dispose of pharmaceutical waste is high-temperature incineration with proper emission controls after “take-back” strategies; however, many countries do not have such incineration facilities or have not implemented “take-back” strategies (Bean et al., 2016; World Health Organization, 1999). In fact, some pharmacies around the world refuse to accept unused medications or discouraged this practice (Tong et al., 2011). These circumstances encourage the practice of un-ecofriendly methods, such as the disposal in a regular waste container with other common garbage (Tong et al., 2011), ending in municipal solid waste (MSW) and disposed of in landfills.

A landfill is an area of land where waste is deposited to avoid any contact with the surrounding environment, particularly the groundwater (Narayana, 2009). Landfills are classified into three categories: i) Open landfills are widely used in developing countries and their operation simply consists of dumping the waste in open land; ii) semi-controlled landfills are designated sites where the dumped waste is compacted and a topsoil cover is added frequently, and; iii) sanitary landfills are well-constructed and managed landfills that have the capacity to intercept and treat the leachate, they are mainly used in developed countries (Narayana, 2009; Nyagah et al., 2020).

Their economic advantages and ease of operation make landfills the main destinations of wastes from many countries (Eggen et al., 2010; Renou et al., 2008; Sabour et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020), such as China, where is their main method to deal with waste (B. Zhou et al., 2017), and Poland and Taiwan, where more than 90% of the urban wastes are disposed in landfills (Renou et al., 2008). Furthermore, the landfill method is also used after performing other waste treatments intended to minimize the waste volume (Ghasemi and Yusuff, 2016); for example, the residual ash from incineration can be landfilled.

The extensive use of landfills combined with the increasing pharmaceutical consumption and the high tendency of disposing them with the regular garbage has led to high levels of pharmaceuticals in MSW. Pharmaceuticals have been detected at concentrations of up to 8.1 mg/kg within MSW (Musson and Townsend, 2009). Once pharmaceuticals are discarded in landfills, may undergo degradation, adsorption, or exit the landfill through the leachate (Musson and Townsend, 2009; Nyagah et al., 2020). Masoner et al. (2014) analyzed fresh leachate samples collected from 19 landfills, which contained a complex mixture of contaminants of emerging concern including pharmaceuticals and plant/animal sterols in a concentration range of 100−1,000,000 ng/L. Similarly, other studies have reported high concentration levels of pharmaceuticals in different landfill leachates (Peng et al., 2014; Sui et al., 2017).

Despite the development of modern designed landfills, capable to collect and treat the leachates (Narayana, 2009; Nyagah et al., 2020), limited removal efficiencies of pharmaceuticals are observed after leachate treatments (Lu et al., 2016). Recently, Yu et al. (2020) evaluated the environmental impact of a well-constructed and managed landfill, equipped with advanced leakage prevention and leachate collection. Their observations indicated a considerable impact in nearby groundwater but a negligible contribution of pharmaceutical waste in surrounding surface water (Yu et al., 2020).

Besides discarded into MSWs, pharmaceutical waste is usually flushed into the sewer system through the toilet or sink. In addition, pharmaceutical compounds or their metabolites excreted through the human body also reach wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), leading to high concentrations of pharmaceutical pollutants in their effluents.

Typically, WWTPs consist ﻿of a set of biological and physicochemical treatments, applied in three main stages: primary sedimentation, secondary treatment, and final sedimentation (Gracia-Lor et al., 2012; Sim et al., 2010). Conventional wastewater treatment methods involve filtration, sedimentation, coagulation/flocculation, chlorination, biodegradation, membrane filtration, adsorption process with a wide variety of carbon materials, and ultraviolet irradiation, among others (Crini and Lichtfouse, 2019; Oluwole et al., 2020). These processes are mainly effective in ﻿removing conventional pollutants, such as biodegradable organic material and nutrients; however, they are not sufficient enough ﻿to remove pharmaceutical products, even at low concentrations (Kümmerer, 2008; Sim et al., 2010). Some studies have reported the presence of pharmaceutical products on influents and effluents from WWTPs (Gracia-Lor et al., 2012; H. Q. Liu et al., 2017; Nebot et al., 2015; Sim et al., 2010), and in wastewater sludge (Ekpeghere et al., 2017; Radjenović et al., 2009), demonstrating that conventional WWTPs cannot completely eliminate pharmaceutical pollutants. This is of great relevance since as presented in Table 2, pharmaceutical wastes produced in almost all stages of the lifecycle of a pharmaceutical product are improperly discarded and eventually reaching wastewater treatment plants, where are not completely removed. In addition, treatment systems and sewage distribution systems may experience leakages into the aquatic environment caused by overflows, overcapacity, and system failures on decaying infrastructures (Daughton, 2007; Shah, 2016). Aging infrastructures and growing populations significantly increase the probability of underground leakages (Daughton, 2007).

For comparison purposes, Table 3 shows the characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages of the conventional treatment systems. In addition, the main pharmaceutical wastes treated by each conventional system are also presented.

In summary, although incineration seems to be an effective method for treating pharmaceutical waste, or that landfilling is widely popular, or that WWTPs are operated with advanced engineering, none of the conventional methods completely eliminate the environmental hazard of pharmaceutical waste (Hoyos et al., 2008). In this context, failure/inefficiency of these conventional methods, in addition to direct discharges, lead to the presence of pharmaceutical contaminants in aquatic environments ( Fig. 3). In this manner, remediation processes with advanced materials for the decontamination of water are essential to overcome environmental and health impacts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots on the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants in water
CDs and their application in photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: Scientific studies related to carbon dots, a novel type of carbon nanomaterial with a size below 10 nm and quasi-spherical shape, have been increasingly published because of their fascinating properties like their biocompatibility, optical stability, easy surface modification, water-solubility, photoluminescence, and simple and inexpensive nature (B. Chen et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2019). The carbon core of CDs can be amorphous, nanocrystalline, or a combination of both (Luo et al., 2020), and according to their intrinsic structure are classified in graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). GQDs present single-layer or multilayer crystalline nanoparticles with conjugated π-electron graphene layers that exhibit quantum confinement (Chen et al., 2019), and an average crystal lattice size of 0.24 nm (Semeniuk et al., 2019). CQDs also exhibit quantum confinement; however, their crystal lattice has an interlayer difference of 0.34 nm, the same spacing observed in graphite (Semeniuk et al., 2019). Finally, CNDs are nanoparticles with an amorphous structure consisting of aggregated or crosslinked monomers or linear polymers; therefore, they do not exhibit quantum confinement (Chen et al., 2019). Since carbon dots present a complex structure with many variations depending on their precursor and synthesis methods, more classifications can be found (Xia et al., 2019).

CDs present many interesting properties, but of relevance are their photoluminescence, high capacity for sunlight absorption, up-conversion photoluminescence properties, and their effective electron transfer (Chen and Bai, 2020), which make them excellent candidates for its use in photocatalysis. Heterogeneous photocatalysis is defined as an energy conversion process through accelerated reactions by a synergetic effect of a solid photocatalyst (semiconductor or metal) and light (Han et al., 2018) with the aim of interact with reactants contained on a fluid. The general mechanism of the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants by photocatalysis can be described with the following reactions (Kar et al., 2021):

When the photocatalyst absorbs photons energy from light, the electrons in the valence band (VB) are excited, causing their movement into the conduction band (CB), which leaves a positive hole (h⁺) (Eqs. 1 and 2). After the photogeneration of these charge carriers, free radicals are generated (Eqs. 3 and 4), which undergo further reactions producing CO2, H2O, and other degraded products (Eq. 5 and Eq. 6) (Kar et al., 2021; Trapalis et al., 2016).

Photocatalysts should be able to absorb light on a wide spectral range and efficiently separate the photogenerated charge carriers. However, many of the conventional photocatalysts currently used, have not overcome their low efficiency of the utilization of sunlight and the high recombination rates from the photogenerated charge carriers (Han et al., 2018; Variar et al., 2021). In this regard, although carbon dots do not possess intrinsic photocatalytic activity, their unique electronic and optical properties such as their strong light absorption, up-conversion photoluminescence, photoexcited electron transfer ability, tunable bandgap, and inhibition of eˉ/h⁺ recombination, have an important role in the construction of highly efficient CDs-based photocatalytic systems (Ren et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). As shown in Table 4, the addition of CDs significantly enhances the degradation rate in comparison to their respective pristine photocatalyst.

As presented in Table 5, the enhanced photocatalytic performance resulted from the addition of CDs is mainly attributed to two different mechanisms: (1) Efficient separation of eˉ/h⁺ and (2) up-conversion CDs property. In addition, it has been reported that the enhancement produced by CDs is also attributed to an improved hydrophilicity and their role as photosensitizers by narrowing the bandgap. Regarding the latter one, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) –a semiconductor frequently used in CDs-based photocatalysts– presents a ﻿bandgap of 2.7 eV (Wang et al., 2017); however, the addition of CDs resulted in a bandgap narrowing from 2.25 to 2.62 eV (Liu et al., 2019; Song et al., 2020; F. Wang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019c; Wang et al., 2018b; Xie et al., 2018). Titanium dioxide (TiO2), as another semiconductor commonly used, presets a larger bandgap of ∼3.2 eV (Bokare et al., 2021); however, with the addition of CDs a bandgap narrowing between 2.75 and 2.87 eV have been reported (Chen et al., 2017; Sharma et al., 2018).

A schematic representation of the photocatalytic mechanism and the CDs' role is presented in Fig. 4. Briefly, the excited electrons from VB to CB of the semiconductor photocatalyst can be transferred to the CDs leading to efficient separation of eˉ/h⁺, avoiding the recombination, thus improving the photocatalytic activity. On the other hand, the up-conversion properties of CDs imply an efficient utilization of visible and near-infrared light (500–1000 nm) that can be converted to higher energy wavelengths (325–425 nm), extending the spectral response of the semiconductor photocatalyst, thus improving the degradation of the pharmaceutical pollutants. The comparative evaluation presented in Table 6 shows that CDs-based photocatalysts overcome the drawbacks of conventional photocatalysts (g-C3N4 and TiO2); however, some disadvantages associated with the CDs’ synthesis are still present. For example, additional separation or purification processes to obtain pure and mono-dispersed CDs are frequently required and some CDs’ synthesis methods are not eco-friendly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based photocatalysts for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the role of the CDs and their effect on photodegradation reactions, it is relevant to study their properties. In this context, the synthesis method and the precursor used have a significant effect on the structure of the carbon dots, and therefore also on their properties. As presented in Table 7, most researchers have utilized CDs synthesized by hydrothermal methods (Huang et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2019). This method belongs to the bottom-up approach, which consists of the organization of atoms or molecules until reaching the desired nanostructure using physical or chemical forces. In particular, the hydrothermal method is widely used because of its low cost and easy operation and control of parameters such as temperature and time. Carbon dots synthesized by the hydrothermal method typically uses citric acid as the carbon precursor because of its biocompatibility and low cost (Ren et al., 2020). In catalytic applications, it is common to find the use of CDs doped with heteroatoms, since a decrease in the surface function is presented, facilitating a more convenient transfer of electrons between reactions (Zhang et al., 2020). The most common doping heteroatoms in the reviewed studies are nitrogen (Liang et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019b; Yan et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018b) and sulfur (Shin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019c). N-CDs can efficiently ﻿induce charge delocalization and enhance photoluminescence (Shin et al., 2020). In addition, N-doping induces surface defects of CDs which led to enhanced electronic conductivity and improved adsorption caused by an increase in the surface area (Li et al., 2017). In this manner, carbon dots –either from citric acid or other precursors– are frequently doped with nitrogen, because of their contribution enhancing the photocatalytic activity (Shi et al., 2019). In addition, has been reported that the strong blue emission presented by N-doped CDs from citric acid is due to the presence of derivatives of citrazinic acid (Schneider et al., 2017). Similarly, S-doping can significantly enhance the photoexcitation and electron concentration of CDs (Shin et al., 2020). Surprisingly, applications on wastewater remediation by CDs synthesized from top-down approaches were rarely found. J. Chen et al. (2016) synthesized carbon dots from starch by a chemical oxidation method with the aim of removing cefradine. ﻿Briefly, carbonized starch was dissolved in distilled water and refluxed with acetic acid and hydrogen peroxide for 2 h at 120 ˚C. The chemical oxidation method is advantageous for large scale production, added to the fact that requires inexpensive equipment; however, it requires the use of strong acids or base (Chan et al., 2018).

Different methods have been used for the CDs synthesis, which strongly influences on their properties and performance; however, their effects are complicated to analyze because of the many variables involved, such as types of reagents, concentrations, process temperature, reaction time, among others. CDs are frequently incorporated on semiconductors such as g-C3N4 (Jin et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Song et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017, 2018a; Wu et al., 2020) and titanium dioxide (TiO2) (Chen et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2018; Shin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019b; Zeng et al., 2019). The high chemical and thermal stability presented in g-C3N4, as well as its appropriate bandgap (2.7 eV), are some of the reasons this semiconductor is widely used in photocatalysis reactions (Chen and Bai, 2020; Yan et al., 2016). However, its photocatalytic performance is significantly limited by its low surface area, insufficient visible-light adsorption, and low quantum efficiency (Chen and Bai, 2020). Similarly, despite its and its low cost, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is limited on a UV irradiation range caused by a bandgap of ∼3.2 eV (Bokare et al., 2021). In this manner, when these materials are modified with CDs, significant improvements occur in terms of broadening the light adsorption wavelengths, inhibition of eˉ/h⁺ recombination, and higher number of ﻿active reaction sites (Bokare et al., 2021), resulting in a better photocatalytic performance. With the same purpose, CDs have been also combined with other semiconductors, such as goethite (α-FeOOH), bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr), cerium dioxide (CeO2), and bismuth molybdenum oxide (Bi2MoO6).

Different approaches to prepare CDs-based composites have been performed. From the analyzed studies presented in Table 7, the method most frequently used is calcination. As a representative work, Zeng et al. (2019) prepared CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst for the degradation of ciprofloxacin, using the calcination method. Briefly, CDs were added in a TiO2/ethanol suspension, which was later vaporized at 75 ˚C, followed by calcination in ﻿a muffle furnace at 300 ˚C for 3 h. The synthesis method is critical for photocatalytic performance. Wang et al. (2017), compared the photocatalytic activity of CDs/g-C3N4 prepared by two different approaches: a composite photocatalyst prepared by a polymerization method and a physical mixture of both materials. They concluded that the synergetic catalytic effect only occurs with the CDs/g-C3N4 prepared by polymerization (Wang et al., 2017).

The association between the methods and the resulting properties of the synthesized CDs-based composites still needs more clarification. In this regard, some studies have been focused on understanding the effect of the synthesis methods for various carbon-based photocatalysts (Hendrix et al., 2019). However, to our knowledge, there is no work associated with the synthesis methods of CDs-based photocatalysts, which could be relevant, since some of the properties of carbon dots still under debate (Gan et al., 2016).

Among the different types of CDs, CQDs were used most widely to enhance photocatalytic ability (Huang et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020); however, GQDs have shown the same capacity (Bokare et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2016), while studies using CNDs were rarely found. In addition, some studies did not specify the type of CDs used to construct their CDs-based photocatalyst. This finding evidence: (i) the need to evaluate the influence that the structure of CDs has on their role in enhancing the photocatalytic degradation, and (ii) the need to fully characterize CDs when they are used for any application to identify the type of CDs and possible differences related to their structure.

From our literature review, CDs-based photocatalyst on pharmaceutical pollutants degradation have been more frequently applied in the degradation of antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin and tetracycline. This could be due to the wide administration of this type of pharmaceutical products and the global concern of the environmental and health impacts caused by their presence in the aquatic environment. However, different pharmaceutical products are presented in wastewater, typically forming complex mixtures of pollutants. From our review, only a few works evaluated the performance of CDs-based photocatalysts in the degradation of different pharmaceutical products. In this regard, the CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst prepared by Wu et al. (2020), outstands for its non-selective oxidative capacity to photodegrade various pharmaceutical molecules such as naproxen, indomethacin, ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin, and diclofenac. Nevertheless, the degradation efficiency varied depending on the pharmaceutical molecule; indomethacin, naproxen, and ciprofloxacin were almost completely degraded, while the degradation efficiency of norfloxacin and diclofenac was lower than 80% (Wu et al., 2020). Similarly, Jin et al. (2021) reported a degradation rate of diclofenac, ﻿naproxen, and triclosan of 93.8%, 83.7%, and 71.7%, respectively using a CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relevant parameters and their effects on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: In previous studies on CDs-based photocatalyst, different variables are associated with their photocatalytic performance ( Fig. 5). For example, Liu et al. (2019) intensively analyzed the effect of the content of CDs added into the photocatalyst. In their study, different amounts of CQDs were added into g-C3N4 by a polymerization method. All the composite preparations were evaluated through photocatalytic tests using diclofenac as a representative pharmaceutical pollutant. Similar methodologies were applied for several researchers (Chen et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2021; Shin et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2019). Interestingly, all studies reviewed here coincide that the quantity of CDs is critical for the appropriate performance of the photocatalyst. In general, the addition of CDs enhances the degradation rate, regardless of the semiconductor used, the type of CDs, the synthesis method, or the pharmaceutical product to be degraded. This enhancement is only observed until a maximum CDs concentration is reached since the excess content of CDs leads to the opposite effect. In this regard, researchers concluded that the inhibition by an excess amount of CDs is caused by a reduction of ﻿active sites acting as recombination centers to further inhibit the generation of reactive species; in addition, light absorption might be blocked by excess content of CDs (Guo et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2019).

Typically, the optimal pharmaceutical pollutant degradation efficiency is reached with a CDs concentration of 0.5–4.0 wt%, added on a typical semiconductor (g-C3N4 or TiO2) (Guo et al., 2017; Su et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2016). Few studies have reported their optimal results outside of the range of 0.5–4.0 wt%. For example, Jin et al. (2021) reported the optimal diclofenac degradation performance of their CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst with CDs concentration as low as 0.2 wt%; while Zeng et al. (2019) reported the optimal ciprofloxacin degradation performance of their CDs/TiO2 with a CDs concentration of 6 wt%. These differences might be attributed to factors associated with the pharmaceutical pollutants or differences in the parameters during the photodegradation tests.

A similar pattern was observed with the effect of the photocatalyst dosage. As a representative work, Jin et al. (2021) investigated the diclofenac degradation efficiency of CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst in the range of 0.4 – 1.5 g/L. They found that increasing the CDs-based photocatalyst dosage from 0.4 to 1.0 g/L resulted in an enhanced efficiency; however, further increases of photocatalyst led to only a slight enhancement. Therefore, 1.0 g/L was selected as the optimal dosage, considering a significantly high degradation rate and low cost of the photocatalyst. On the other hand, Liang et al. (2020) investigated the photocatalyst dosage effect on a wider range (0.2 – 3.0 g/L) concluding that 0.8 g/L was the optimal concentration of their CDs-based photocatalyst to reach the maximum pharmaceutical degradation since further dosage increases resulted in reducing the degradation rate. A lower amount of photocatalyst results in a fewer generation of eˉ/h⁺, thus a lower degradation rate is expected. Then, if the photocatalyst composite increases, the photocatalytic activity increases as well. However, an excess amount of photocatalyst can inhibit the propagation of photons and combined with a scattering effect by the suspended particles can lead to the decrease of photocatalytic degradation (Zhi Li et al., 2019). These results were also supported by other researchers (Hu et al., 2018; Xiaoyu Jin et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Typically, to achieve optimal performance, the photocatalyst composite should be added between the range of 0.5–2.0 g/L (Chen et al., 2016; Xiaoyu Jin et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2020; X. Liu et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020). Conventional photocatalysts have been used on similar dosage to achieve the optimal degradation efficiency; the optimal pharmaceutical pollutant degradation by TiO2 have been reported around 1.0 – 1.5 g/L (Safari et al., 2015; Palominos et al., 2009), while 1.0 – 1.67 of g-C3N4 photocatalysts are required to efficiently degrade pharmaceutical pollutants (Xu et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2018).

Similarly, the effect of the irradiation source has been evaluated by different research groups (P. Chen et al., 2017; Liang et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020). As a representative work, Wang et al. (2019,b) prepared N-CQD/TiO2 nanosheet with higher surface energy {0 0 1} faceted (NCDs/TNS-001) for the degradation of diclofenac, which was tested under broad-spectrum irradiation (visible light, ﻿near-infrared light, UV light, and simulated sunlight irradiation). The prepared N-CDs/TNS-001 was compared with its counterpart TNS-001 (photocatalyst without CDs). The TNS-001 only presented a weak degradation efficiency under UV light irradiation. In contrast, NCDs/TNS-001 presented a significant degradation efficiency under the broad spectrum. These observations demonstrate the capacity of CDs to absorb visible light and ﻿near-infrared light resulting in the formation of eˉ/h⁺. Then, the up-conversion property from CDs allowed them to transform the ﻿longer-wavelength visible and near-infrared light to higher energy wavelengths of less than 400 nm that could excite the TNS-001 (F. Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b). Interestingly, the up-conversion property of CDs is limited under lower wavelengths (∼350 nm); which explains the weak enhancement observed with CDs addition on g-C3N4 photocatalyst under UV light (F. Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b; Y. Wang et al., 2018b).

The initial concentration of the pharmaceuticals in surface waters and the influents/effluents from WWTPs is highly variable. Pharmaceutical residues are presented at µg/L to ng/L concentrations in surface waters (O’Flynn et al., 2021), while the concentrations of pharmaceutical residues in the influents from several WWTPs ranged from 11.5 to 30.2 µg/L (Sim et al., 2010). Therefore, the way in which the initial concentration of the pharmaceutical pollutant influences the photocatalytic activity was studied by Sharma et al. (2018). They investigated the degradation efficiency of CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst at different initial concentrations of levofloxacin (5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, and 15 mg/L); concluding that at the initial concentration of 5 mg/L, the composite presented better photocatalytic performance. Increasing to 10 mg/L led only to a significant reduction in the photocatalytic performance from 92% to 90%; however, a significant reduction to 84% occurred with an initial concentration of 15 mg/L (Sharma et al., 2018). The decrease in the performance was even more noticeable in the degradation of diclofenac by CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst, constructed by Jin et al. (2021). In their study, a decrease in the degradation efficiency from 100% to 64% was observed increasing the initial concentration from 1 mg/L to 15 mg/L. This parameter has been extensively studied by other researchers (Chen et al., 2016; Jin et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2020; Sharma et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018b; Wu et al., 2020). Overall, these studies demonstrate that high concentrations of pollutants lead to insufficient photon absorption because a competitive effect of photons by the pollutant molecules occurs, resulting in a decrease in the degradation rate.

On the other hand, pH is a highly important factor during photocatalytic reactions for several reasons: (i) pH influence ﻿the interactions between the photocatalyst and the pollutant; (ii) chemistry of pharmaceutical molecules is highly sensitive to variations of pH; (iii) surface properties of photocatalyst are pH-dependent; and (iv) the formation of ﻿reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals can be affected by pH (Kumar et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2019). For example, CDs/porous-g-C3N4 prepared by W. Liu et al. (2019), presented a higher degradation rate of diclofenac on alkaline conditions, caused by improved adsorption of OHˉ affecting the electronegativity of the photocatalyst and its consequent ﻿decrease of the conduction band ﻿potential, which in turn increase ⦁O2ˉ generation. Contrastingly, the ﻿CQDs/α-FeOOH prepared by Huang et al. (2020) exhibited a higher degradation efficiency of tetracycline at pH< 7. The initial pH influenced the surface properties of the CDs-based photocatalyst and the ionization state of tetracycline –protonated, neutral, or monoanionic– depending on the pH value. In this manner, at extremely alkaline conditions, the degradation efficiency was limited by ﻿electrostatic repulsion between the monoanionic form of tetracycline and the photocatalyst surface; as well as the competition effect between OHˉ and tetracycline for active sites (Huang et al., 2020). Similarly, a CDs-based photocatalyst constructed by Kumar et al. (2020), showed a higher chloramphenicol degradation at pH 6 – 7 caused by the electrostatic interactions between the chloramphenicol in ﻿anionic form and the ﻿positively charged photocatalyst. The poor activity of their photocatalyst at extremely acidic and basic conditions was caused by the ﻿similar charge between photocatalyst and pollutant which lead to electrostatic ﻿repulsion (Kumar et al., 2020). Other studies, such as the performed by Sharma et al. (2018) have reported insignificant variations in photocatalytic efficiency by the pH; the highest degradation of levofloxacin (90%) was presented at pH 6, whereas at pH 9 the photocatalytic degradation slightly decreased to 88%, while at pH 4–86.8%. The authors concluded that the optimum amount of hydroxyl radicals was produced at slightly weak alkaline medium improving the degradation efficiency of levofloxacin (Sharma et al., 2018).

From our review, we observed that the optimal pH is highly dependent on the ionization state of the organic compound and the surface properties of the photocatalyst. Photocatalysts should present an excellent catalytic performance within a wide pH range to be considered as good candidates for wastewater treatment.

Pharmaceutical pollutants are contained in water samples, which may also contain inorganic ions. The inorganic ions can affect photocatalytic reactions, interfering with ﻿the generation of active oxygen species or pollutant behavior. Therefore, the effect of inorganic ions is highly dependent on the photocatalytic system. For example, NO3ˉ had almost no effect on the photodegradation of cefradine using a CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite. But, on the photodegradation of chloramphenicol using CDs/reduced graphene oxide/g-C3N4, NO3ˉ ions accelerated the degradation rate because of the formation of ⦁OH radicals under visible light. In this manner, it is necessary to identify the effect on the photodegradation efficiency of the CDs-based photocatalysis to optimize their activity for a wide range of molecules under ambient conditions.

The problems associated with wastewater contamination by pharmaceutical products begin with the improper management of pharmaceutical products. Therefore, a prevention approach must be considered as a starting point. Pollution prevention is typically an unexplored pathway to minimize the environmental impacts of the pharmaceutical industry; however, it is highly relevant to avoid the initial release of pharmaceutical wastes into the environment. In this review, we analyzed the pharmaceutical wastes generated in each stage of a pharmaceutical product. Each stage should be further studied to identify proper measures to prevent the release of pharmaceutical waste. For example, during the manufacturing stage, some preventive measures like optimization of processes to minimize waste production and solvent waste recycling should be considered ( Fig. 6). Regarding the consumption stage, since one of the most important pathways for the release of pharmaceutical waste into the environment is excretion, is relevant to incorporate a cultural perspective to understand how the environment is interrelated with misuse of pharmaceutical products. Moreover, adequate consumption practices would provide great benefits to the prevention of pharmaceutical pollution. Particularly, the collaboration of pharmacists is highly required to encourage rational drug use. Similarly, the pharmacist can be involved in preventive measures during the disposal stage, analyzing the best options for the disposal of pharmaceutical waste and providing newer solutions.

On the other hand, the current contamination by pharmaceutical waste demands also remediation actions. In this respect, CDs have probed their excellent performance on the photodegradation of pharmaceutical pollutants; therefore, CDs have an important role in environmental remediation. In this manner, their synthesis through environmentally friendly, simple, effective and economic methods remains a challenge and is strongly required. Accordingly, waste materials with high carbon content might be excellent candidates as precursors through top-down synthesis methods, which should be further explored. From the studies reviewed here, only a few works presented CDs-based photocatalyst with a non-selective degradation activity. This must be considered because wastewater is typically composed of a mixture of pollutants. In the same regard, the application of such CDs-based photocatalysts in real and complex water systems still needs to be explored.

Other research opportunities are the understanding of reaction kinetics with different pharmaceutical pollutants, the study of the effects of several parameters in order to optimize photocatalytic performance, and a comprehensive understanding of the influence of the structure of carbon dots on their photocatalytic properties.

Current manufacturing, consumption, and disposal practices of pharmaceutical products facilitate the entry of pharmaceutical waste into the environment. This, added to the fact that the processes to treat conventional pollutants are insufficient when dealing with pharmaceutical products, leads to the contamination of aquatic environments and its subsequent environmental and health impacts. As an alternative, remediation processes with advanced materials for the decontamination of water are emerging, such as photocatalysis using CDs-based photocatalyst. The unique electronic and optical properties of CDs cause a synergetic effect during photocatalytic reactions. In this manner, highly efficient CDs-based photocatalytic systems can be conveniently used to completely degrade pollutants present in the water into CO2 and inorganic substances under environmental conditions. In this review, was analyzed the conditions to achieve an optimized performance considering relevant parameters such as the variable pH of wastewater, presence of inorganic ions, initial concentration of pollutants, and the required content of CDs and photocatalyst composites. Finally, new technologies with advanced nanomaterials for the decontamination of wastewater by pharmaceutical products, should not be considered as the solution, but rather as part of an integrated solution that begins with good management throughout the entire lifecycle of the pharmaceutical products.
